title: Molecular deep learning at the edge of chemical space
methods: spectral clustering for OOD data splitting
data_sources: LIT-PCBA (3 targets: ESR1, TP53, PPARgamma; drugdesign.unistra.fr/LIT-PCBA)
ChEMBL v33/v35 (pretraining corpus + CDK1 data, ebi.ac.uk/chembl)

data_scope.labelled_datasets: 33 experimentally annotated bioactivity datasets (29 MoleculeACE + 3 LIT-PCBA + 1 Ames mutagenicity)
data_scope.molecules_per_split: 14,081 molecules per in-distribution and OOD test split after curation
data_scope.pretraining_corpus: ~1.2 million unlabelled drug-like molecules from ChEMBL v33
data_scope.retrospective_screening_library: 1,395,422 unique molecules combined from Asinex, Specs, Enamine (46,048,926 inference entries across 33 targets)
data_scope.prospective_screening_library: ~185,000 molecules per target (PIM1: 185,298; CDK1: 185,336) after physicochemical and kinase-enriching filters
data_scope.wet_lab_compounds: 60 compounds assayed at 10 uM; 8-point dose-response for 6 top hits per target
data_scope.data_access_dates: LIT-PCBA: Aug 2023; commercial libraries: Mar-Apr 2025; CDK1 from ChEMBL v35: Apr 2025

core_conclusion: Molecular ML models fail on OOD molecules precisely where novel drug candidates reside. The Joint Molecular Model (JMM) introduces 'unfamiliarity' — a reconstruction-based metric derived from a jointly trained SMILES autoencoder — that reliably detects distribution shifts across 33 bioactivity datasets and 1.4M screening compounds, outperforming Bayesian uncertainty under strong covariate shift. Prospective wet-lab validation discovered 7 low-micromolar hits for PIM1 and CDK1 kinases, all structurally distant from training data (max Tanimoto <= 0.38), demonstrating that unfamiliarity extends ML-guided discovery beyond the charted edge of chemical space.
why_it_matters: Solves a fundamental reliability gap in molecular AI under distribution shift; bridges generative and predictive modelling via joint reconstruction-prediction training; provides the most systematic benchmark to date (33 datasets, 1.4M molecules); closes the AI-to-bench loop with wet-lab validation; fully open code and datasets.
why_nature: Conceptually novel metric category (neither applicability domain nor Bayesian uncertainty); broad applicability to any SMILES property prediction task; prospective experimental validation raises the bar for computational ML; societal significance for drug discovery; open platform and reusable pretraining corpus.
added_value_to_my_research: Transfer unfamiliarity (joint reconstruction + prediction) to spatial tabular inputs — flag where GeoAI models are OOD before deployment for health/urban policy
Regression-mode unfamiliarity as reliability surface for MGWR/GWR spatial prediction models beyond their training geography
Active-learning loop using spatial unfamiliarity as exploration signal for healthcare facility siting in underserved OOD zones
Spectral-clustering OOD split logic applied to temporal concept drift detection in spatiotemporal mobile-phone / social-media prediction
Urban unfamiliarity layer: combine spatial autoencoder reconstruction loss with multi-source spatial entropy for principled GeoAI reliability mapping

figures.caption: JMM architecture: applicability domain concept, encoder-decoder-classifier with unfamiliarity from reconstruction loss, spectral-clustering OOD split on OX2R dataset

Distribution-shift detection: scaffold similarity, MCS fraction, pharmacophore similarity and predictive performance across 33 datasets; unfamiliarity score distributions for test ID vs. test OOD (n=14,081 mol/split)
Virtual screening on 1.4M molecules: Tanimoto similarity, uncertainty, and unfamiliarity distributions; utopia-point mapping of uncertainty vs. unfamiliarity pan-pharmacologically and for PIM1
Prospective wet-lab validation: compound selection by strategies A/B/C, measured PIM1 and CDK1 inhibition at 10 uM, dose-response curves, IC50 structures of 6 most potent hits
